preFix.title: Willow egg basket
preFix.subtitle: A small round willow basket sized for a dozen eggs
preFix.excerpt: A small round willow basket from brown buff willow rods. The oval base and low straight sides hold a dozen eggs. The project introduces the base, the upsett, and the simple rand weave. A good first basket for anyone new to willow work.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cooke The Basketmaker (1882) for the oval slath base method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the egg basket form and dimensions.

content.text: A willow egg basket is a small oval basket with low, straight sides: around 280 mm long, 200 mm wide, and 80 mm deep. The form uses a
base of six thick base rods, from which a ring of
are upset upright and woven in a simple rand. The low sides mean the weaving is fast; the oval shape is introduced at the slath before the base rods are opened.

Brown buff willow is the standard material for a first basket. Brown buff is boiled in its skin and dried; it is the most forgiving of the willow preparations, neither as brittle as white (stripped) willow nor as heavy as green willow fresh from the bed. Rod sizes needed: six base rods at 4 to 5 mm diameter and 350 mm long; twelve side stakes at 3 to 4 mm diameter and 350 mm long; a bundle of fine weavers at 2 to 3 mm diameter and any length for the sides. All

Submerge all rods in the soaking trough for one hour. After soaking, wrap in a damp cloth or hessian and leave for thirty minutes to mellow: the moisture moves from the surface through to the core. Test flexibility by bending a butt end gently; it should bend through 90 degrees without cracking. Rods that crack were not soaked long enough, or were very old stock.
Take three base rods and pierce the centres with the bodkin to make a slot. Thread the other three base rods through those slots to form a cross: this is the

. Tie the slath by weaving a fine withy in a pairing weave around the sets of three for two rounds, then open the base rods out to individual spokes. Continue pairing until the base measures 280 mm long by 200 mm wide. The long axis is formed by allowing extra rounds on the two end spokes to widen the oval.

Cut a pointed stake for each base spoke and push it into the base beside each spoke using the bodkin to open the path. Kink each stake sharply at the base edge by pinching it against the bodkin; this helps the
stand upright. Tie them together at the top temporarily with a length of withy. Weave the
: four rows of three-rod waling, worked in both directions to balance the stress. The upsett rows pull the stakes upright and set the basket shape.

Use a simple rand weave: one weaver, in front of one stake and behind the next, all the way round. Join new weavers at the side of a stake, overlapping the old rod by four stakes before cutting. The sides are 80 mm high. Finish the weaving section with another four rows of three-rod waling to stabilise the top before the border.

A three-rod plain border: take the first three stakes. Kink each one over to the right using the bodkin to open a path behind the next stake. Thread each kinked stake behind the stake to its right and in front of the stake to its right-right. Continue working around the basket, threading each stake through in sequence until all stakes are woven in. Trim all stake ends flush with secateurs.

attrs.fix: Complete at least two full rounds of tied pairing before opening the base rods to individual spokes. The initial tie holds the slath flat.
attrs.cause: Not enough rounds of pairing before opening the slath spokes
attrs.symptom: The base buckles in the centre

attrs.fix: Re-soak the bundle. If individual rods crack, set them aside and continue with the flexible ones. Do not force a cracked rod; it will weaken the structure at that point.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
